did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning architecture combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) for automated skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. Specifically, it employs a pretrained VGG16 backbone for feature extraction and a lightweight transformer module to capture global spatial relationships, trained on publicly available dermoscopic datasets to differentiate between benign and malignant lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a hybrid deep learning architecture (CNN and Vision Transformer) for skin cancer classification. It details a specific methodology including data preprocessing, model training on public dermoscopic datasets, and reports experimental results such as accuracy (~80%) and AUC (high 0.8 range). Although published in conference proceedings (ICMSCI-2026), it contains original technical contributions and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: AI-Powered Image Classification for Skin Cancer using CNN and Vision Transformer
journal: Proceedings of the Second International Conference on Multi-Agent Systems for Collaborative Intelligence (ICMSCI-2026)
brief_summary: This study presents an automated skin cancer classification system using a hybrid deep learning architecture that combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs). The model utilizes a pretrained VGG16 backbone for local feature extraction and a lightweight transformer module to capture global spatial relationships from dermoscopic images. It was trained and evaluated on publicly available dermoscopic datasets to differentiate between benign and malignant lesions. The experimental results demonstrated approximately 80% test accuracy with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) in the high 0.8 range. The hybrid framework showed improved classification reliability compared to standalone architectures, offering potential as a clinical decision-support tool for early diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two classes in the binary classification task to differentiate from malignant lesions.

Used as one of the two classes in the binary classification task to differentiate from benign lesions. The paper discusses BCC, SCC, and Melanoma as types of skin cancer but does not report specific per-class metrics for these subtypes, focusing instead on the binary malignant vs. benign distinction.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to differentiate between benign and malignant skin lesions using dermoscopic images. The model outputs a probability score via a sigmoid activation function, with a decision threshold of 0.5 used for final class prediction. The task is explicitly defined as 'binary lesion classification' and 'skin cancer detection' (binary). Metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: Public dermoscopic datasets and clinician-submitted cases
datasets_used.description: A combined dataset consisting of curated public dermoscopic images and clinical cases submitted via a secure web-based portal (MCQ submission). The paper notes the dataset has limited size and diversity.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid CNN-ViT model, with class balancing techniques (oversampling, class weighting) applied to address imbalance between benign and malignant samples.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor metrics (AUC, accuracy) for early stopping and checkpointing, and to calibrate decision thresholds.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation on a held-out test set to report sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC.
use.other: External validation on independent datasets is recommended for future work but not reported as completed in this study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To maintain class proportions across training, validation, and test sets, ensuring minority subgroups and different skin phototypes remain represented.
To address class imbalance between benign and malignant samples during training.
To emphasize harder cases during training (used optionally).
To prevent overfitting by halting training when improvements plateau.
To adaptively fine-tune learning progress and optimize convergence.
To improve prediction robustness by averaging predictions across multiple augmented versions of each image.
To calibrate probability outputs for use as interpretable risk scores for triage purposes.
To understand error distribution and analyze error patterns in detail.
To calibrate decision thresholds and optimize clinical utility by maximizing sensitivity while keeping the false positive rate at a clinically acceptable level.
To guarantee reproducibility of data splitting, augmentation, and preprocessing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics such as accuracy (~80%) and AUC (high 0.8 range) without statistical significance testing.

Hybrid CNN-ViT (VGG16 backbone + Vision.validation_accuracy: typically between the high 70s and low 80s

Public dermoscopic datasets and clinicia.notes: The model achieved approximately 80% test accuracy with an AUC in the high 0.8 range. Loading the best validation-based checkpoint often yielded slightly higher AUCs. The study notes that some misclassifications occurred among lesions with similar visual features and that predictions were sensitive to image quality (noise, blur, low contrast). The paper did not report specific per-class metrics (sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) as numerical values, though it states these were prioritized for evaluation. No statistical significance tests (p-values) were reported.

architecture_details.backbone: VGG16 (CNN) for local feature extraction (texture, color, boundaries)
architecture_details.transformer_module: Lightweight Vision Transformer with 1x1 convolutions for tokenization, learnable positional embeddings, and one or two self-attention blocks with residual connections and LayerNorm
architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of Global Average Pooled (GAP) CNN features and Mean Pooled Transformer outputs
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected layers with dropout (p=0.5) and L2 regularization, followed by a single neuron with sigmoid activation for binary classification
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout (p=0.5) in dense head, L2 weight decay, dropout within attention blocks

training_details.loss_function: Binary cross-entropy or Focal loss (for class imbalance)
training_details.transfer_learning_strategy: Initial freezing of lower VGG16 blocks, followed by gradual unfreezing of top convolutional layers
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Class weighting, oversampling of malignant examples, extensive augmentation
training_details.augmentation: Random rotations, contrast enhancement, hair removal, color normalization, optional lesion segmentation, test-time augmentation

preprocessing_techniques: Removal of visual artifacts (hair and ruler markings)
Optional lesion segmentation to focus on region of interest
Removal of duplicates, low-quality images, and mislabeled entries
Normalization according to VGG16 input requirements
Real-time data augmentation (random rotations, flips, brightness and contrast adjustments, scaling, elastic deformations)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during on-the-fly training augmentation to introduce variability.

Applied in challenging cases by averaging predictions across multiple augmented versions of each image to improve prediction robustness.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning framework combining a VGG16 convolutional backbone with a Vision Transformer (ViT) demonstrates strong potential for accurately distinguishing between malignant and benign skin lesions.
The model achieved approximately 80% test accuracy with an Area Under the Curve (AUC) in the high 0.8 range, indicating robust generalization capabilities.

The hybrid architecture effectively leverages both local texture features (via CNN) and global contextual relationships (via ViT), improving classification reliability compared to standalone architectures.

The system offers enhanced interpretability through attention mechanisms and Grad-CAM heatmaps, which confirmed that the model focuses on relevant lesion regions rather than background artifacts, supporting its potential as a clinical decision-support tool.
The model exhibits fast inference times, suggesting suitability for real-time clinical use such as assisting primary care physicians with early screening and triage.

Limitations include misclassifications among lesions with similar visual features and sensitivity to image quality (noise, blur, low contrast), highlighting the need for high-quality image acquisition in deployment.

The framework's performance is constrained by the limited size and diversity of the current dataset, and future work recommends external validation on independent datasets and stratified analyses to assess generalization and demographic bias.

Future improvements may involve expanding dataset diversity, incorporating advanced explainability techniques like Grad-CAM for visual justifications, and addressing class imbalance to further enhance real-world clinical utility.
important_remarks: The study relies on a limited dataset size and diversity, combining public dermoscopic datasets with clinician-submitted cases, which may introduce selection bias and limit generalizability to broader populations.
External validation on independent datasets is recommended but not reported in this study, raising concerns about potential domain shift and demographic bias.

The model's performance is sensitive to image quality; inputs with noise, blur, or low contrast resulted in reduced confidence scores, highlighting a deployment constraint regarding image acquisition standards.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; results are presented as descriptive metrics (approx. 80% accuracy, high 0.8 AUC) without significance testing.

Specific per-class metrics (sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score) were prioritized for evaluation but numerical values were not explicitly reported in the text, limiting detailed assessment of class-specific performance.
The paper does not report the total number of samples or the specific distribution across training, validation, and testing sets, preventing assessment of sample size adequacy.
Misclassifications were noted among lesions with similar visual features, indicating limitations in distinguishing visually ambiguous borderline cases.
The work was published in a conference proceeding (ICMSCI-2026) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may imply less rigorous scrutiny compared to journal publications.
